notes.anchor: C. CASSIVS Q. S. D. M. CICERONI
notes.note: The sender is C. Cassius Parmensis (the poet and tyrannicide), not Cassius Longinus, with whom the manuscripts confuse him; "Q." abbreviates quaestor, his rank with the eastern fleet. S. D. is salutem dicit, "sends greetings."

notes.anchor: the greatest consular has surpassed his own greatest consulship
notes.note: A compliment to Cicero: as a consularis (ex-consul) leading the resistance to Antony in 43 BC he has now outdone even his celebrated consulship of 63 BC, when he crushed the Catilinarian conspiracy.

notes.anchor: your toga has been more fortunate than all men's arms
notes.note: An echo of Cicero's own boast cedant arma togae ("let arms yield to the toga"): his civilian statesmanship has achieved more than any general's campaign.
"Our Cassius" is C. Cassius Longinus, here named in the third person by the letter's writer Cassius Parmensis; he is the commander besieging Dolabella at Laodicea in Syria.

notes.anchor: at a javelin's cast away \textit{[Greek: paltōi]}
notes.note: Cassius Parmensis drops into Greek (paltōi, "by a javelin"), measuring the distance between the two camps by a single spear-throw; the Greek tag is preserved in the text.

notes.anchor: wheat is at three tetrachms a measure
notes.note: A tetrachm was a four-drachma silver coin; three tetrachms for a measure of wheat signals famine prices inside Dolabella's blockaded camp.

notes.anchor: He has pitched camp before the town of Laodicea
notes.note: This is Laodicea ad Mare on the Syrian coast (modern Latakia), where Dolabella took refuge and was besieged --- not the inland Phrygian Laodicea in the province of Asia.
Crommyuacris was a promontory on the north coast of Cyprus where the fleet lay; the letter is dated from there on the Ides (13th) of June 43 BC.
